data.title: Scared Money Won't Make Money: Still Bullish On These Beaten-Down Buys
data.description:
Introduction

When it comes to investing, many of us likely wonder what will happen when we invest our hard-earned money. When buying equities, the goal is to gain wealth, not lose it. Although losing money is something you often can't avoid.

However, you can mitigate this by investing in safer, blue-chip stocks, or even placing your money into Exchange-Traded Funds. But even then, your money is neither safe nor guaranteed.

To paraphrase Peter Lynch: If you invest and are not prepared to lose 50% of your money, then you shouldn't be investing.

Moreover, doing your own due diligence is best to help stomach volatility. Regarding these two stocks, both have seen their fair share of headwinds, with both down double-digits in the past year.

But that's where the potential to see strong wealth creation is generated, buying beaten-down, fundamentally sound companies whose prices seem disconnected. In this article, I discuss two dividend stocks, despite increased uncertainty, I remain bullish on.

Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE)

The first stock on the list is a REIT that's gotten a lot of love from analysts' here on Seeking Alpha, me included. I last covered the stock in-depth back in February, where I urged investors to not try to time the bottom and just buy the dip.

And the dip has kept on dipping, with the stock down an additional 29.85% since that article published. For the year, ARE is down more than 37% at the time of writing.

One reason for the dip is that ARE is classified as an office REIT; a sector that still has not fully recovered from the pandemic in 2020.

With many businesses still incorporating a hybrid work schedule, many office properties remain vacant today. Secondly, ARE faces a real risk from the integration of artificial intelligence.

According to some, more than 60% of jobs in advanced economies will be impacted by AI. And a lot of these jobs are the ones that rent office spaces from REITs like ARE. And until there's more certainty surrounding the potential disruption in jobs, Alexandria and others will likely continue to see volatility.

Throw in a slowing economy, potential stagflation, and a possible recession, and they are also likely to see further downside before things get better. However, I remain bullish on Alexandria.

For one, management is taking advantage of the price disconnect by buying back shares. In the first quarter, ARE led the way with $231 million worth. This shows management is forward-thinking, and that they maintain capital discipline despite heightened uncertainty.

In their latest quarter, they revised AFFO guidance for the year, lowering it to reflect slower leasing activity. This is now expected in a range of $9.16 - $9.36, down from $9.23 - $9.43 prior.

However, the REIT did manage to beat analysts' estimates during the quarter by $0.02. FFO was $2.30 while revenue also beat by a solid $3.9 million, coming in at $758.2 million. However, FFO did decline from the previous quarter by $0.09 and from $2.35 the year prior.

Same-store NOI was down 3.1% as a result of headwinds from slower leasing activity as well as expiring leases. On a cash-basis, same-store NOI was actually up 5.1%.

But fundamentally, ARE remains sound as 87% of their revenue comes from large, investment-rated tenants. They also collected 99.5% of rent in the quarter.

And as previously mentioned, Alexandria's balance sheet gave them flexibility. Not only for share repurchases, but to weather increased volatility. Leverage was solid at 5.9x and they had robust liquidity totaling $5.3 billion. Leverage did increase from 5.2x at the end of 2024, and this is something investors should keep a close eye on going forward.

Office REITs are known to operate with higher leverages than other REIT peers, but I like to see this below 6.0x, preferably in the mid 5's or below.

On the other hand, their 7.3% dividend yield is also enticing. And this is well-covered by their cash flows. In Q1, ARE's payout ratio was a comfortable 57%.

In the chart below, with the exception of this year, ARE had continued to grow its AFFO solidly.

On top of that, the REIT offers some serious upside over the long term if they can return to their historical multiple. At the current price, Alexandria Real Estate has a forward multiple of just 7.52x, well-below their historical average of 12.97x.

Currently, Wall Street rates them a buy, with nearly 39% upside from the current price. While significant upside won't be likely in the near-term, I do think they see solid upside in the next 12 to 24 months. You also collect a 7.3% well-covered yield while you wait.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH)

Second on the list is the healthcare giant, UnitedHealth Group. The company has faced a slew of headwinds in the past year that has pushed the price down more than 38%, relatively similar to Alexandria's 37%.

But as previously mentioned, Scared Money Don't Make Money and now may be the time to buy to see long-term capital appreciation.

UNH has had leadership changes, faced negative comments from the President, and even faces lawsuits from shareholders & the Department of Justice.

Medicaid & Medicare cuts have also impacted the company. But fundamentally, they remain sound. Like ARE, the company has also been repurchasing shares, albeit on a lesser scale. In their latest quarter, shares outstanding stood at 918 million, down from 922 million at the end of 2024.

However, this shows management remains disciplined with their liquidity. It also shows their balance sheet, gives them flexibility amidst heightened uncertainty.

The company also has an A credit rating and roughly $3.6 billion in short-term debt. Long-term debt actually declined slightly from the end of 2024 from $72.3 billion to $71 billion. One of the few positives.

They also had a strong cash position, with $30.7 billion in cash & cash equivalents. So, from a balance sheet standpoint, UnitedHealth is in good shape.

And despite their headwinds, they actually saw some growth year-over-year, albeit less. Revenue was up from $99.8 billion to $109.6 billion, while adjusted EPS was up from $6.91 to $7.20.

But like Alexandria, UnitedHealth was forced to revise their guidance as well. EPS is now expected to retract to a range of $26 - $26.50. Revenue is still expected to increase from $400.2 billion to $450 billion - $455 billion.

UNH's latest performance was hindered by their Optum Health segment. Memberships impacted revenue as the healthcare giant saw reduced spending from cuts. They also saw plan exits in certain markets, likely due to high prices.

The segment revenue, margins, and earnings all declined from the previous year. Revenue was down from $26.7 billion to $25.3 billion, while earnings declined from $1.899 billion to $1.614 billion. As a result, margins were down from 7.1% to 6.4%.

But the company's dividend remained safe and well-covered. They raised it by a solid 5.3% from $2.10 to $2.21. This is nearly half of last year's 11.7% raise from $1.88, an obvious sign of the challenging times.

Management stated they planned to mitigate risk by focusing on operating costs and taking advantage of AI to improve operating efficiency. Going forward, I expect the company to face headwinds and increased volatility over the near term. But long-term, I see significant upside in the next 12 to 24 months, similar to Alexandria.

For starters, interest rates are likely to be lower, so consumers should see some relief from high prices. And two, I expect them to return to high double-digit earnings growth over the long term.

You can see, over the past decade, UnitedHealth has comfortably covered its dividend, giving them ample room for future increases and flexibility to navigate tough times like now.

Like Alexandria Real Estate, Wall Street also rates them a buy and expects significant upside from the current price of $303 a share at the time of writing.

Again, I wouldn't expect significant upside in the near term as the market looks for more stability and growth in earnings as well as results from their pending lawsuits. And for now, all of this poses risks for the company to see further downside.

Bottom Line

Both Alexandria Real Estate and UnitedHealth Group are two stocks that are currently dealing with a slew of issues, placing pressure on their share prices.

And while they are likely to see increased volatility as investors wait for clarity surrounding interest rates, trade, and a potential recession, now is the time to put your money to work and likely see significant upside with these out of favor stocks.

If you have a higher risk tolerance and are looking to put money to work, then consider these two high-quality dividend stocks for your portfolio

data.title: NervGen Pharma: Risky, Yet Promising With Big Upside
data.description:
Thesis

NervGen Pharma Corp. (OTCQB:NGENF) is a clinical-stage biotech firm trying to carve out a niche in nerve repair and regeneration. Its lead drug, NVG-291, is aimed at spinal cord injuries, one of the complex, less-explored areas of medicine that still has a massive gap in effective treatments.

Meanwhile, the market’s been riding high on expectations for NVG-291, but the company’s future boils down to upcoming clinical results, making it a very high-risk, high-reward bet. In my research, I argue that despite the uncertainties, NervGen’s momentum and market potential makes it a tempting option for investors who can handle the risk.

NervGen Pharma's Promising Trial Data Breaks New Ground

Earlier this month, NervGen released topline results from the chronic cohort of its Phase 1b/2a trial for NVG-291, a peptide designed to repair spinal cord injuries and in patients with cervical-level spinal cord injuries, the drug produced a statistically significant increase in motor evoked potential amplitude in the first dorsal interosseous muscle. In plain terms, that's the muscle that indicates whether the hand still works and patients who got the drug showed a strong, measurable improvement in nerve signal strength, while those on placebo barely changed, and the results were statistically meaningful. To be specific, the treatment group’s MEP amplitude jumped from 6.207 to 18.773; meanwhile, the placebo group crept up from 6.527 to 7.760. The p-value was 0.0155, good enough to move the discussion from speculative biology to clinically relevant impact.

In the meantime, the second co-primary endpoint, MEP in the tibialis anterior, a leg muscle, didn’t meet significance. But based on my research: no one expected it to. NervGen wasn’t trying to get people back on their feet; it was trying to get their hands working again. For patients with cervical injuries, regaining the ability to grasp, feed themselves, or use a phone often outweighs mobility. Clinicians, like Dr. James Guest, routinely cite upper extremity function as the most life-altering gain these patients can hope for. So if NVG-291 improves hands, even a little, the clinical significance lands far harder than the stats might suggest.

Backing up the main results were some encouraging, secondary findings. Take the GRASSP prehension subtest, for example, a test that essentially measures how well someone can pick up and move things with their hands. Here, the treatment group improved by an average of 3.7 points, while those on placebo managed just 0.4. Statistically, the difference wasn’t enough to set off bells, p=0.1416, but half the treated group improved by at least 4 points, compared to just 10 percent of the placebo group. The Nine-Hole Peg Test, which involves placing tiny pegs into tiny holes and is a standard way to measure fine motor control is also tilted in favor of the treatment, again, not by headline-grabbing margins, but enough to suggest that something might be going on.

Meanwhile, the treatment was well tolerated. No serious adverse events, no dropouts. The most common side effects, as it turns out, were mild to moderate reactions right where the injection went in, which is about what you'd expect with a peptide drug, really. That alone may not sell shares, but it shores up regulatory standing, which, in biotech, is arguably more valuable.

Even more telling is what NervGen chose not to do. When its second candidate, NVG-300, showed encouraging preclinical activity in models of ischemic stroke and SCI, the company made a hard call: discontinue preclinical ALS work. Not because ALS isn’t worth pursuing, but because the signal wasn’t strong enough. Instead of chasing the market with marginal science, a temptation almost no early-stage biotech resists, NervGen concentrated on the path with clearer data and nearer-term returns.

NervGen has entered the scene just as the nerve repair and regeneration market is picking up steam, a space where most of the spotlight has landed on surgical fixes like grafts, implants, and hardware meant to shock the nervous system into working order. That’s the turf of companies like Axogen, Inc. (AXGN) and Boston Scientific Corporation (BSX), who’ve more or less made themselves at home there. NervGen, on the other hand, is taking a less conventional route, focusing on something called the PTPσ signaling pathway, a well-documented but neglected mechanism that tends to block nerve repair.

Even more notably, the company is deliberately targeting chronic injuries, cases 1 to 10 years post-injury, instead of focusing exclusively on the subacute window (20–90 days post-injury), where most drug candidates live. This decision runs against the grain of conventional drug development, which tends to follow biological optimism like a compass. But NervGen appears to grasp something rather important: people with chronic spinal cord injuries, while notoriously difficult to help, can see life-changing improvements from even the smallest return of function. These are people largely abandoned by the drug development pipeline. Any improvement, however incremental, can alter their lives. That’s a very high-risk strategy, definitely, but one with an outsized potential payoff, both clinically and commercially, which I'll get to shortly.

Layered over this is a larger shift in how the regulatory and technical apparatus around biotech is evolving. Tools like AI and 3D bioprinting are altering preclinical modeling, giving companies like NervGen the ability to simulate, refine, and validate drug behavior before it ever hits a patient. At the same time, the FDA is gradually warming to new kinds of evidence, particularly biomarker-driven endpoints. This matters. Motor evoked potentials (MEPs), the very electrophysiological readout that NVG-291 improved in early trials, are increasingly seen not as functionally relevant surrogates. In other words, what NervGen is measuring might soon become the benchmark for approval. That would collapse timelines, reduce trial sizes, and lower the bar for market access.

NervGen Pharma: Growth, Risk, and a Questionable Price Tag

To get straight to the point, in the first quarter of 2025, NervGen spent $4.1 million in operating cash, which brought its cash and investments down to $14.5 million, a 16% dip from the $17.3 million it started the year with. To help plug the gap, the company raised $1.3 million through its at-the-market equity program and warrant exercises during the same period. A further $2.5 million came in shortly after, bringing the total near-term capital raised to $3.8 million. Still, even with the cash injection, the total haul barely buys NervGen a few quarters of breathing room before the walls start closing in again.

At a sustained burn rate of $4 to $5 million per quarter, consistent with the trailing twelve-month burn of $12.3 million, NervGen has between three and four quarters of runway left, which may carry them through topline data but certainly not through late-stage clinical development.

The strain is magnified by an upward drift in costs: research and development (R&D) spending for Q1 2025 came in at $3.1 million, slightly above the $3.0 million from the same period last year, mostly attributable to higher compensation costs, including non-cash stock-based compensation, driven by expanded headcount. Though you might argue that this is totally reasonable for a company scaling toward clinical inflection.

And, inevitably, we arrive at the bottom line where NervGen posted a $3.9 million net loss for the quarter, or $0.06 per share, up from $2.3 million, or $0.04 per share, the year before. This larger loss includes $1.5 million in non-cash expenses, mostly from amortization, stock-based compensation, and foreign exchange swings. A $2.0 million non-cash gain related to warrant derivatives helped offset the losses, although less than the $2.4 million derivative gain reported in the same period last year. These derivative gains, are rooted in mark-to-market shifts in financial instruments rather than actual operational performance. In practical terms, it means the company’s reported net loss is already being masked to a degree by paper gains with no impact on its real cash position.

Giving it further thought, you could argue that complicating the outlook was NervGen’s decision to halt the preclinical development of NVG-300 in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) which I mentioned earlier. The strategic pivot undeniably narrows its pipeline as ALS is one of the most publicly recognized and commercially significant neurodegenerative indications, and removing it from the near-term roadmap reduces both addressable market and diversification. NVG-300 now exists primarily as an early-stage asset with potential but no immediate path forward, unless the company can secure a development partner.

From what I can tell though, none of this, however, has dampened investor enthusiasm, at least not yet. On June 11, 2025, NervGen’s stock closed at $2.85, landing the company with a market cap of $206.6 million and an enterprise value of $195.5 million. What makes these figures so striking to me is how wildly they diverge from the company’s actual fundamentals.

NervGen’s price-to-book ratio is 209.63, which is not only 8,758% above the sector median of 2.37, but also more than 1,000% above its own five-year average of 17.65x. Essentially, the market’s betting that NVG-291, NervGen’s lead candidate, will come through with solid, statistically significant results in its chronic cohort trial, paving the way for Phase 3, partnerships, or even a buyout.

Even more notably, the stock’s technical momentum has validated that investor conviction. NervGen’s one-year price return of 72.35% stands in stark contrast to the S&P 500’s 12.39%, and even more dramatically to the healthcare sector’s –19.77%. The six-month return is 60.07%, more than 600% above its five-year average performance of just 8.48%, while the three-month return of 48.44% similarly dwarfs the sector’s negative performance over the same window. Whether driven by institutional front-running of topline data or retail momentum chasing, capital is flowing in at a velocity that suggests a consensus: something important is coming.

In the meantime, NervGen continues to bleed money at a pace that virtually guarantees another financing event unless near-term data changes the capital narrative. There is no evidence of margin compression or revenue leverage because there is no revenue. And there won’t be unless NVG-291’s trial results are both favorable and fast-tracked.

Final Takeaway

In my view, there is, undeniably, a broader industry context that makes NervGen’s aspirations plausible, if not yet probable as the global nerve repair and regeneration market is expected to grow from $11.5 billion in 2025 to $32.4 billion by 2034, a steady annual growth rate of 12.2%. So far, no drug has stepped up to lead, especially in spinal cord injury. If NVG-291 works, NervGen could grab a first-mover advantage in a market hungry for answers. And if that happens, then yes, a $200 million market cap could be seen in retrospect as absurdly cheap.

But that future value is already priced in. The market is already assigning a probability-weighted discount rate that assumes NVG-291 will deliver. Should the chronic cohort data confirm efficacy, particularly in restoring hand and arm function for cervical SCI patients, NervGen will most likely rally, possibly attracting partners or suitors. But, as we've all seen in the past again and again, if the data falters, whether due to insufficient statistical significance or underwhelming clinical relevance, then the current 209x book value becomes indefensible, and the correction will be swift.

Given NervGen's strong momentum and massive outperformance compared to its five-year average and the healthcare sector, along with a major data catalyst on the horizon and access to a multi-billion-dollar market, I’m calling this a Strong Buy. It's high-risk for sure, but the upside here is too tempting. The stock is priced in for success, but not ridiculously so, considering how much it could gain if the clinical results live up to the hype.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oklo: Valuation Is A Leap Of Faith
data.description:
Introduction

I have a very positive view on nuclear energy, as outlined in various articles where I covered Cameco (CCJ) Great Asset, Talen (TLN) Exaggerated Fears, and BWX Technologies (BWXT) A Defensive Option in SMR, to name a few. The small modular reactor (SMR) idea seems a very good one, given speed to market, and I bought a small position in Oklo (NYSE:OKLO) in its SPAC debut, thinking it would languish for several years as its technology and business plan resulted in meaningful revenue and cash flow. However, the stock has far exceeded the best expectations of analysts and faces significant challenges in attaining more upside.

Opportunity Meets Reality

Rather than dive into the validity of Oklo's technology and integrated build-and-run model, which includes nuclear fuel manufacturing, I looked at the current set of consensus estimates to see if the stock is worth its current price and when it may see further upside.

The consensus data, some nine analysts (Capital IQ & Bloomberg), forecast meaningful revenue in 2029 and positive EBITDA in 2031 based on the current letter of intent (LOI) for power purchase agreements of some 2,100 MW, which backs into 42 plants at an average of 50 MW each. Time to market, the ability to build units from Alaska to Texas, may be complex to achieve. At the same time, these LOIs can disappear if others show up sooner.

Oklo stated it can have its first until in 2027, having attained the permits, funding, and supply chain. The most challenging task is manufacturing the fuel HALEU (High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium), and it may actually be a better business than building SMRs

CapEx and Cash Burn

Another factor in the Oklo business model is that it is capital intensive; it will need to spend around US$100m per 50MW or US$5,000 per-MW, which is expensive but perhaps offset by low build time, which is vital as an operator. The consensus estimates that over US$2bn in capex is needed to drive revenue, which may result in US$6bn in debt by YE34. The recent US$400m capital raise will likely be a recurring event.

Valuation

The great thing about start-ups or pre-operating companies is that valuations take on a highly theoretical aspect based on huge payback sometime in the future. However, when companies finally produce revenue, cash flow, and eventual earnings, the market has something real to measure, and in many cases, reality smashes theory.

I value Okla at 10x EV/EBITDA when it reaches US$1.6bn in EBITDA in 2033. This multiple takes into consideration the higher-risk business model of building, owning, and operating SMRs.

In the case of Oklo, if it meets estimates some nine years from now, it may be worth US$145, which is under a 14% annual return with plenty of dilution and operating risk. In my view, the current price seems to require a leap of faith.

Risk to the Sell

The market can easily continue to buy shares into infinity based on perceptions of grandeur, and one will miss out. It may be acquired or alter its build and run business model to licensing technology, or even better, sell HALEU to the 100s of future SMRs.

Conclusion

I rate Okla a Sell. The key earlier investors have exited, the business model requires a high level of capex, and if everything goes right, the shares may be worth double in 2034, a leap of faith I would not take.

data.title: Simulations Plus: Further Downside Is Likely (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Simulations Plus' (SLP) share price plummeted after the company reported preliminary Q3 FY25 results that fell well short of expectations. While soft biopharma market conditions and Simulations Plus' relatively large services business mean that current weakness is not surprising, the company sounded optimistic on its Q2 earnings call in early April. This suggests that market conditions have shifted considerably over the past 2 months.

I previously suggested that declining margins and a high valuation were problematic. I also pointed out that while Simulations Plus' commentary was positive, organic growth was decelerating. As a result, I questioned whether the company would be able to generate strong returns for investors. Even though Simulations Plus' share price is now down almost 50% since then, I am still not bullish on the stock due to its valuation and a reliance on acquisitions and volatile services revenue to generate growth.

Market Conditions

The biopharma market has been under pressure for several years, in large part due to high-interest rates and a tight financing environment. Government policy has also been a headwind, although recent developments at the FDA have been positive. For example, the FDA is phasing out animal testing requirements, starting with monoclonal antibody therapies, with methods like AI and organoid testing used as a replacement.

While government funding has also been a concern, Simulations Plus has suggested that there is minimal risk from exposure to NIH and academic funding.

On its second quarter earnings call, Simulations Plus suggested that the operating environment was stable. While customers are cautious, demand for Simulations Plus’ software continues to increase. Renewal rates have been fairly stable and Simulations Plus continues to land new customers, despite the funding challenges facing some smaller biotech companies. Services demand has been under pressure though, with customers delaying the start of some projects.

Simulations Plus Business Updates

Simulations Plus recently reported disappointing preliminary Q3 FY25 results, which left investors blindsighted after the positive commentary on the Q2 earnings call in early April and strong full-year guidance. Simulations Plus expects 19-20 million USD revenue in the third quarter, compared to an estimate of roughly 22.8 million USD. Full-year revenue is now only expected to be in the 76-80 million USD range, an increase of 9-14%. This is well down from prior guidance of 90-93 million USD revenue and is particularly soft when considering the sizable contribution from the Pro-ficiency acquisition. Simulations Plus largely attributed weakness to market headwinds causing budget reductions and project cancellations and delays. While Simulations Plus hasn't broken out the results, softness appears to have been driven by its services segment.

Simulations Plus continues to introduce new versions of its software with broader capabilities and improved performance. The company is also collaborating with USC on the development of new AI drug discovery offerings. While this should be supportive of software growth longer term, it does little to address the company's current issues.

SLP Stock Financial Analysis

Simulations Plus generated 22.4 million USD revenue in the second quarter, an increase of 23% YoY. While total revenue growth was strong, organic growth was only 5%.

Software revenue increased 16% YoY, representing 60% of total revenue, with 8% organic growth. Simulations Plus' Quantitative Systems Pharmacology business was a growth driver in the quarter, with QSP revenue up 89% YoY. QSP revenue is lumpy though due to the size of individual licenses (upwards of 500,000 USD). Simulations Plus' renewal rate was steady in the quarter based on accounts. It declined based on fees though, which was attributed to a large customer that delayed renewal.

Services revenue increased 34% YoY, but there was no organic growth. Services bookings were strong in Q2 though, and as a result, Simulations Plus' backlog increased 13% YoY to 20.4 million USD.

Simulations Plus was guiding to 90-93 million USD revenue in FY25, representing 28-33% growth. Full-year revenue is now only expected to be in the 76-80 million USD range, an increase of 9-14%. Pro-ficiency generated 15 million USD revenue in 2023 and Simulations Plus was expecting Adaptive Learning and Insights and Medical Communications to contribute 15-18 million USD in FY25. As a result, organic revenue is likely to be down by double digits in FY25.

Simulations Plus' gross margin was 59% in Q2, with an 81% software gross margin and a 25% services gross margin. The recent decline in gross margins has largely been driven by amortization expenses. Simulations Plus has also moved some general and administrative expenses to the services cost of revenue line item in connection with a business unit reorganization. The Pro-ficiency acquisition is also a drag as its software gross margins were around 80%, versus 90% for Simulations Plus. Pro-ficiency’s business was also more heavily weighted towards lower margin services.

Simulations Plus' operating margins and free cash flows are also under pressure, and this situation is likely to worsen in coming quarters given expected revenue weakness. Simulations Plus is targeting a return to 35-40% adjusted EBITDA margins, but this will only likely see GAAP operating margins return to the mid teens. The company's decision to cut dividends in 2024 was also a negative signal in terms of near-term profitability expectations.

Conclusion

The biosimulation market is highly fragmented and underpenetrated, providing Simulations Plus with both organic and inorganic growth opportunities. The company's software also has high renewal rates and pricing power, which are supportive of unit economics and deserving of a relatively high revenue multiple. The market is weak at the moment though, which is negatively impacting Simulations Plus' services business, and it is not clear when this situation will change.

Strategic M&A remains a focus area, even though Simulations Plus now has little cash on its balance sheet. While Simulations Plus may be able to make acquisitions at attractive prices in the current environment, dilution is a risk.

Despite being down over 75% from the peak, Simulations Plus' revenue multiple remains relatively high given the high contribution from volatile and relatively low margin services. As a result, I believe that Simulations Plus' stock could still have further downside, particularly as the Pro-ficiency acquisition is lapped and comparable periods become much tougher.

data.title: Oil Soars Amid Israeli Attacks On Iran, Upgrading FENY
data.description:
Friday the 13th delivered bad news on the geopolitical front. Israel launched strikes on Iran’s nuclear sites, causing turmoil across global markets. Equities were off between 1-2% initially, which are not overly dramatic moves, but massive gains were seen in oil.

WTI crude oil soared by as much as 14% overnight Thursday into this past Friday. As of the premarket on Friday morning, the Energy sector was up by more than 8%--which would be its best week since September 2022.

Today, given the macroeconomic developments, I’m refreshing my view on the Fidelity MSCI Energy Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FENY). I had a hold rating last February, but I am upgrading it to a buy. I see the fund as having a low valuation, and with some renewed momentum, I see upside potential.

Moreover, it’s clear that treasuries are no longer a risk-off and safe-haven asset when energy prices rise, making oil & gas equities all the more appealing if we are in a new, heightened geopolitical environment.

Energy ETF Set to Soar 8% This Week, Best Since September 2022

Treasury Yield Rise Amid Geopolitical Upheaval

President Trump Urges Dealmaking

According to the issuer, FENY seeks to invest at least 80% of its assets in securities included in the MSCI USA IMI Energy 25/50 Index. The index is a modified market capitalization-weighted index that captures the large-, mid-, and small-cap segments of the USA market. The issuer employs a representative sampling indexing strategy to manage the fund.

Shares of FENY are down 3.5% since my Q1 analysis, though with today’s implied advance, shares are likely about flat compared to a 0.3% loss in the S&P 500 over that time. With tepid returns over the last handful of months, it’s not surprising to see that its assets under management has dropped from $1.5 billion to less than $1.3 billion.

Its annual expense ratio is very low at just 8 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 3.1%--almost two percentage points above that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been weak lately, earning the fund a poor D ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Although after the Energy sector’s best week in almost three years, that rating may improve imminently. FENY is, of course, a risky fund, given its concentrated portfolio and high standard deviation, with the post-Liberation Day volatility period still close in the rearview mirror.

Still, the fund is highly liquid with its ownership of US large-cap Energy stocks—the average daily volume is north of 1.7 million shares, while its median 30-day bid/ask spread is just three basis points, per Fidelity.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star ETF by Morningstar plots on the upper-left portion of the style box, indicating its bent to the US large-cap value space. Just 8% of FENY is considered growth, while 54% is actually classified as SMID cap.

Overall, the price-to-earnings ratio is low at 15.2x, and the long-term earnings growth rate is very strong now at 14.5%, resulting in a PEG ratio of barely above 1x, which is a significant improvement from just four months ago, supporting my upgrade.

FENY: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Of course, FENY’s two largest positions are the major-integrated oil names: Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX). Keeping close tabs on both the fundamentals and technicals with those two heavyweights is key.

Earnings season is still more than a month away, so the focus will be on geopolitics and how both WTI and Brent oil prices evolved as we head into the second half of the year. In all, FENY’s top 10 assets make up almost two-thirds of the allocation.

FENY: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal perspective, we are actually in what’s normally a very quiet period. The June through July stretch has historically produced small price returns when averaging the past 10 years of data.

FENY: Lukewarm Seasonal Trends

The Technical Take

With oil soaring in the wake of the Israel-Iran conflict news, FENY comes into the geopolitical event in poor form, but there are some green shoots to weigh. Notice in the chart below that with today’s implied move, the fund will leap above its long-term 200-day moving average, currently just above the $24 mark. I see resistance at an old gap down from around Liberation Day at the $25.50 mark. Above that, we find the multi-year highs at $27 that could be in play over the second half of 2025.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph—it has already notched its best mark since January, preceding the upside price move. We often see that in technical analysis—momentum turns before price. But with a high amount of volume by price up to $26, the bulls still have some wood to chop to break the broader sideways trend. I do like, though, that there was a big volume spike on a recent up-day. On the downside, support is just below the $23 level.

FENY: Poised to Lift Above its 200 dma, $25.50 First Stop, Bullish RSI Trend

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on FENY. I like Energy stocks today given the geopolitical risk rise. Furthermore, it’s clear that treasuries don’t offer the same safety characteristics as they did in previous cycles, so having a slice of US oil & gas stocks that can benefit from rising WTI and Brent prices is helpful. FENY also trades at a cheaper price compared to a few months ago.

data.title: Adobe: This Was The Last Reset - Upgrading To A Strong Buy On AI
data.description:
I last covered Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE) in mid-December of last year, and initiated the stock with a buy after the stock pulled back on weaker-than-expected guidance for the quarter. Little did I know that was merely the beginning of a downward trend, not only for Adobe, but the market at large, as the tariff fears neared.

Investment Thesis

I think the worst is behind us, and investors are better positioned to add around current levels. Thus, I'm updating the stock to a strong buy around $400 where the EMA21 is currently acting as strong support.

Why now? The company reported a double beat on the 2Q25 quarter Thursday after the bell, and boosted its full-year revenue and earnings outlook. Going forward, I'm optimistic about the rapid uptake of Adobe's AI-focused offerings. I remain wary of how far AI monetization is; according to the company's CEO, Shantanu Narayen:

It's very early in terms of the AI monetization, but we're very advanced in terms of how much innovation we've delivered.

The stock remains in the red year to date, but is up around 22% since its early April lows when the stock traded at $340 to trade at $413 currently. While the technicalities point to more downside ahead, with EMAs not stacked bullishly, I think a pullback would give investors the chance to load up on weakness.

When I last covered the stock, I was betting that Adobe would be a good long-term buy, and my sentiment stands strong. I continue to think the company has a unique competitive moat: Adobe's Creative Cloud + Acrobat + Experience Cloud combo, which, I think, is unmatched in terms of product breadth and integration. While near-term AI revenue may be lacking, Adobe's foundational strength, sticky product ecosystem, and AI rollout should position it for a rebound in sentiment and valuation in the 2HFY25. The quarter's results solidify my optimism.

Earnings Snapshot

Adobe reported 2Q25 on Thursday after market close, and the stock traded lower despite the company exceeding expectations across the board, with guidance raised due to increased AI traction. For the quarter, the company reported an EPS of $5.06, above the consensus of $4.97, and GAAP EPS of $3.94, which was also above the consensus of $3.87. Revenue marked a record high and came in at $4.87 billion, against the consensus of $5.80 billion, up 11% year over year.

Looking forward, 3Q25 is expected to come between $5.15 and $5.20, above estimates of $5.11, and revenue is expected to range between $5.875 billion and $5.925 billion, with the mid-point above estimates of $5.88 billion.

For FY25, the company expects revenue between $23.5 billion and $23.6 billion, above estimates of $23.45 billion, and EPS between $20.5 and $20.7, also well above estimates of $20.39. According to Dan Durn, the company's executive president and CFO:

As a result of us driving strong performance in the first half of the year, we are pleased to raise Adobe's FY25 total revenue and EPS targets... We continue to invest in AI innovation across our customer groups to enhance value realization and expand the universe of customers we serve.

The company exited 2Q with RPO at $19.69 billion and had purchased 8.6 million shares for the quarter. Narayen said that the company's AI "innovation is transforming industries, enabling individuals and enterprises to achieve unprecedented levels of creativity." The Digital Media segment was up 11% year over year to $4.35 billion, while the Digital Experience segment was up another 10% during the same period to $1.46 billion.

Adobe has been the focus of debate on whether AI will have a disruptive effect on traditional software industry leaders. Firefly, according to the earnings call, had traffic up 30% quarter over quarter (for the first time) and paid subscriptions around double during the same period. The app is driving new user acquisitions, has been used to generate over 24 billion pieces of content, according to Durn, up from 20 billion in March. According to the president of the digital media business, David Wadhwani:

Excitement for and adoption of generative AI innovation, such as Generative Fill in Photoshop, Generative Remove in Lightroom, Generative Expand in Illustrator, Generative Extend in Premiere Pro, video generation in the Firefly App and production workflows in Firefly Services continues to accelerate.

Narayen reiterated this and said that while AI-influenced ARR is already generating billions, Adobe's AI-first products, like the Acrobat AI Assistant, Firefly App and Services, and GenStudio for Performance Marketing, are outperforming expectations and are on pace to exceed the $250 million ARR target by the end of fiscal 2025.

Valuation and Price Movement

I think valuation is attractive at current levels. Adobe's forward PE is at 20, 40% below its 5-year average and around 12% below the sector median at 22.9. Year to date, forward PE was down around 7%, on par with Google (GOOG) but significantly above Microsoft (MSFT), which was up 13% during the same period.

Adobe's EPS growth year over year is 45%, above the sector's at 11%, and revenue growth is also at 10%, again above the sector's at 7%. On profitability, the net income margin of 31% again exceeds the sector median of 4%.

EV/Sales ratio is at 7.4, above the sector median at 2.9, and the price/sales ratio of 7.5 also exceeds the sector median at 2.9. On the technical side, EMAs are stacked bearishly, with EMA21 at $408, above EMA50 at $401.8, but both are below EMA200 at $439. The stock is down around 10% over the past year, and up only 1.7% over the past five years, as it peaked back in 2021 at around $688 and never revisited these levels again. I think the stock is poised for another run in the mid-term, and would advise investors to use the near-term pullback that's forming as a buying opportunity, especially more so now as management continues to double down on AI-driven innovation and product integration. I believe most of the downside has already been priced in following two consecutive quarters of sell-offs, and think the stock is now more poised for a comeback. RSI is currently at 58, recovering from overbought territory back in mid-May.

What's Next?

With a reset in expectations, accelerating AI adoption, and a valuation reset, I believe Adobe offers an attractive risk-reward profile heading into 2HFY25. Hence, I'm upgrading the stock to a strong buy before the market catches up.

data.title: Immunovant: Navigating The Complexities Of The FcRn Landscape (Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Immunovant, Inc.'s (IMVT) stock is off almost 50% since my rating upgrade to Buy in April 2024. Back then, I was encouraged by Phase 1 data from its subcutaneous anti-FcRn antibody, IMVT-1402. The investigational drug is designed to promote deeper IgG reductions.

In my last update, I noted that the anti-FcRn market is expected to exceed $10 billion in the coming years due to its broad potential across autoimmune conditions such as generalized myasthenia gravis (gMG) and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP). Immunovant planned to initiate “4-5 potentially registrational programs” within a year. Given that FcRn inhibition is already a proven mechanism, I figured that its path to the market could be swift.

Notably, the competition in the anti-FcRn market was accelerating. Beyond Argenx’s (ARGX) FDA-approved VYVGART, there are a few investigational treatments.

However, I believed Immunovant’s Phase 1 data “de-risked” the asset and that the stock’s weakness was an opportunity for long-term investors.

The following article revisits Immunovant to see if anything has changed to merit such a drastic reduction in its valuation.

Recent Developments

Immunovant’s pioneering FcRn inhibitor, batoclimab, is in late-stage development for Graves' disease (GD) and Thyroid Eye Disease. While batoclimab is great at reducing IgG, it’s known to cause dose-dependent increases in cholesterol and LDL-C. Immunovant is more interested in going through the motions with batoclimab to provide proof of concept for IMVT-1402 and showcase its differentiation. There is no expectation that batoclimab will compete meaningfully with VYVGART. In fact, Immunovant halted batoclimab despite positive Phase 3 results in MG and promising data in CIDP due to competitive pressures and an unfavorable safety profile. Notably, there are now three FcRn inhibitors, including UCB’s RYSTIGGO and Johnson & Johnson’s (JNJ) IMAAVY (approved April 2025).

Batoclimab does have Phase 3 Thyroid Eye Disease (TED) to look forward to. There is just one approved biologic (TEPEZZA) for TED. Amgen’s (AMGN) TEPEZZA generated a whopping $1.9 billion in 2024, testifying to the market opportunity in TED. Argenx, too, is in Phase 3 for TED. So, it makes sense for Immunovant to pursue batoclimab in TED. At the very least, it will inform them as to whether or not to pursue the indication for IMVT-1402.

Meanwhile, the company has advanced IMVT-1402 in a potential registrational trial in GD (December 2024), rheumatoid arthritis (RA), MG, CIDP, and cutaneous lupus erythematosus (CLE). Many of these trials leverage insights gained from batoclimab’s program. Lastly, Immunovant intends to start a study in Sjögren’s disease (SjD) this Summer. Interestingly, there are no approved treatments for SjD. So, pursuing a condition like SjD avoids the crowd that others like MG and GD come with.

So, Immunovant is aiming to be “best-in-class” in the anti-FcRn market. The most important feature is deeper IgG reduction. Recall that the Phase 1 trial data revealed IgG reductions approaching 76% for IMVT-1402. This compares favorably to VYVGART, which will typically show 60%-70% IgG reductions across clinical trials. Immunovant believes that deeper IgG reductions will cause better clinical outcomes, but this is yet to be substantiated. Immunovant is also developing IMVT-1402 for self-administration via the YpsoMate autoinjector. This makes the subcutaneous administration easier for patients, but is unlikely to deter patients from using VYVGART HYTRULO, which can also be self-administered.

Interestingly, Immunovant, initially established by Roivant Sciences (ROIV), is becoming more like Roivant. Roivant maintains ~58% of Immunovant’s shares, and its President and Chief Operating Officer, Eric Venker, was recently appointed as Immunovant’s new CEO. Immunovant has since narrowed the IMVT-1402 pipeline from ten to six indications, indicating alignment with Roivant’s portfolio-level optimization.

Financial Health

As for financials, Immunovant does not generate any revenue and incurs substantial R&D expenditures. In the year ended March 31, 2025, R&D and G&A expenses were a staggering (relative to what I am used to seeing) $360.917 million and $77.235 million, respectively. Immunovant utilized $375.874 million in operating activities during that same time. This implies a monthly cash burn of roughly $31 million. As of March 31, Immunovant had $713.9712 million in cash and cash equivalents. So, this gives the company about two years of cash runway. Please note that this is just an estimate based on historical data and does not account for future changes (such as OpEx reductions). However, my estimate aligns with the company’s forward-looking estimate of 2027.

Conclusion

To wrap up, it’s not surprising to see that Immunovant’s stock has struggled a bit, especially within the context of today’s macro environment for the biopharmaceutical market with tariffs and proposed price cuts. The company has made notable progress with IMVT-1402, advancing into a few potentially registration-enabling trials. Meanwhile, it continues to learn from batoclimab. While costly, insights from batoclimab’s trials could pay off in the long run. Immunovant’s increasing alignment with Roivant is probably both good and bad. It offers resource pooling and strategic guidance, but Immunovant’s priorities are no longer its own.

The crux of the matter here is the challenge associated with demonstrating IMVT-1402’s superiority over existing therapies and others in development. Otherwise, it is unlikely to win a fair share in any autoimmune indication. And despite its stock being down nearly 50% since last year, its $2.7 billion market capitalization implies big expectations. Should, for example, IgG reductions fall into the low 70s or high 60s, what then for IMVT-1402? What is its major differentiation? Moreover, we must consider the costs associated with advancing IMVT-1402. The R&D investments are exorbitant and are only justified in the rare event that IMVT-1402 becomes a multi-billion-dollar “platform” therapy across a variety of indications.

All in all, like the market, I am more cautious of Immunovant, meriting a tempered rating of Hold in the context of a barbell portfolio.
